name: Painter’s Paradox
summary: Painting infinitely many stripes or regions in finite area challenges intuition about measure.

name: Berry’s Paradox
summary: The phrase ‘the smallest natural number not nameable in under eleven words’ refers to a number it claims cannot be named.

name: Meno’s Paradox
summary: If one knows what one is looking for inquiry is unnecessary, but without any knowledge inquiry seems impossible.

name: Socratic Paradox
summary: Claims like ‘I know that I know nothing’ appear self-undermining yet express a coherent stance on knowledge limits.

name: Non-Voting Paradox
summary: Abstentions or turnout changes can flip collective outcomes in counterintuitive ways.

name: Bertrand’s Paradox (Chord)
summary: Different ‘natural’ procedures for choosing a random chord in a circle yield different probabilities.

name: Boxes Paradox
summary: Box and urn setups where conditioning on information reverses naive probability judgments.

name: Moore’s Paradox
summary: Statements like ‘It is raining, but I don’t believe it is’ appear absurd though not logically contradictory.

name: NPV vs. IRR Paradox
summary: Projects can have conflicting rankings under net present value and internal rate of return criteria.

name: Dividend Paradox
summary: Dividend policy appears to affect firm value despite theoretical results suggesting irrelevance under ideal conditions.

name: Thomson’s Lamp
summary: A lamp toggled infinitely many times in finite time raises the question of its final state.

name: Double Negatives Paradox
summary: Phrases with double negatives can express affirmation or nuanced attitudes, defying simple logical mapping.

name: Zeno’s Paradoxes
summary: Arguments like Achilles and the tortoise claim motion is impossible due to infinitely many required steps.

name: Jevons Paradox
summary: Efficiency improvements can increase total resource use when they lower effective cost and spur demand.

name: Tea Leaves Paradox
summary: Tea leaves collect in the center of a stirred cup despite naive centrifugal expectations.

name: Fitch’s Knowability Paradox
summary: If all truths are knowable, it appears to follow that all truths are in fact known.

name: Drinker Paradox
summary: In any non-empty bar there is a person such that if that person is drinking, everyone is drinking.

name: Paper Balloon Paradox
summary: Light paper balloons can rise in air in ways that seem to contradict simple weight-based expectations.

name: Elevator Weight Paradox
summary: Apparent weight changes in an accelerating elevator can feel paradoxical without Newtonian analysis.

name: Paradox of Thrift
summary: Individual attempts to save more can reduce aggregate income and total saving in the economy.

name: Aristotle’s Wheel Paradox
summary: Two concentric wheels seem to trace equal path lengths despite having different circumferences.

name: False Friends Paradox
summary: Similar-looking words across languages can mislead speakers into incorrect interpretations.

name: Paradox of Hedonism
summary: Directly pursuing pleasure can undermine its attainment, whereas indirect pursuit can be more effective.

name: Irresistible Force / Immovable Object
summary: The coexistence of an irresistible force and an immovable object appears logically impossible.

name: Liberal Paradox
summary: Minimal individual rights can conflict with Pareto efficiency in social choice frameworks.

name: Ultimatum Game Paradox
summary: Subgame-perfect equilibrium predicts acceptance of any positive offer, yet low offers are routinely rejected.

name: Liar Paradox
summary: The sentence ‘This sentence is false’ is true if and only if it is false.

name: Russell’s Paradox
summary: The set of all sets that do not contain themselves leads to contradiction in naive set theory.

name: Grelling–Nelson Paradox
summary: The predicate ‘heterological’ seems to be heterological if and only if it is not.

name: Curry’s Paradox
summary: Self-referential conditionals can trivialize certain logical systems, deriving any statement.

name: Hilbert’s Hotel
summary: An infinite hotel can accommodate additional guests even when fully occupied by rearranging rooms.

name: Banach–Tarski Paradox
summary: A solid ball can be partitioned into finitely many pieces and reassembled into two balls of the original size.

name: Gabriel’s Horn
summary: A surface of revolution can have finite volume but infinite surface area.

name: St. Petersburg Paradox
summary: A game with infinite expected value elicits only modest real-world willingness to pay.

name: Monty Hall Problem
summary: Switching doors in a game show setup doubles the chance of winning, contrary to many intuitions.

name: Simpson’s Paradox
summary: A trend present in multiple groups can reverse when the groups are combined.

name: Berkson’s Paradox
summary: Conditioning on a common effect can induce spurious negative correlations between independent variables.

name: Prosecutor’s Fallacy
summary: Confusing the probability of evidence given innocence with the probability of innocence given evidence.

name: Birthday Paradox
summary: In a group of only 23 people there is about a 50% chance that two share a birthday.

name: Two-Envelope Paradox
summary: Naive expected-value reasoning suggests always switching envelopes, leading to apparent inconsistency.

name: Parrondo’s Paradox
summary: Two losing games can be alternated or mixed to produce a winning outcome.

name: Ellsberg Paradox
summary: Preferences over ambiguous urns violate expected-utility axioms but match common attitudes to ambiguity.

name: Allais Paradox
summary: Choice patterns over lotteries violate the independence axiom of expected utility theory.

name: Insurance–Gambling Paradox
summary: People buy insurance and gamble simultaneously, showing risk-averse and risk-seeking behavior in different domains.

name: Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem
summary: No rank-order voting system can satisfy a small set of fairness criteria when there are three or more options.

name: Condorcet Paradox
summary: Collective majority preferences can be cyclic even when individual preferences are transitive.

name: D’Alembert’s Paradox
summary: Ideal-fluid theory predicts zero drag on certain bodies, contradicting everyday experience of resistance.

name: Gibbs Paradox
summary: Entropy of mixing seems discontinuous between identical and slightly different gases.

name: Loschmidt’s Paradox
summary: Microscopic reversibility appears to conflict with macroscopic irreversibility.

name: Maxwell’s Demon
summary: A hypothetical demon seems able to violate the second law of thermodynamics by sorting molecules.

name: Twin Paradox
summary: A traveling twin ages less than a stay-at-home twin in special relativity.

name: EPR Paradox
summary: Entangled particles exhibit correlations that challenge classical locality and realism.

name: Olbers’ Paradox
summary: An infinite static universe of stars should produce a bright night sky, not darkness.

name: Black Hole Information Paradox
summary: Black hole evaporation seems to destroy information, conflicting with quantum unitarity.

name: Mpemba Effect
summary: Under some conditions hot water appears to freeze faster than cold water.

name: Sorites Paradox
summary: Small changes seem unable to transform a heap into a non-heap, yet repeated small changes do so.

name: Ship of Theseus
summary: A ship gradually replaced plank by plank raises questions about numerical identity over time.

name: Newcomb’s Problem
summary: One-boxing and two-boxing choices both appear rational under different principles of decision.

name: Prisoner’s Dilemma
summary: Mutual defection is a dominant-strategy equilibrium but yields worse payoffs than mutual cooperation.

name: Sleeping Beauty Problem
summary: Different updating rules yield conflicting credences for a repeated-awakening experiment.

name: Trolley Problem
summary: Trading one life for many yields conflicting judgments across different intervention variants.

name: Paradox of Analysis
summary: Analyses must be both correct and informative, yet correctness seems to require synonymy, which seems uninformative.

name: Paradox of the Ravens
summary: Observing a non-black non-raven appears to confirm ‘All ravens are black.’

name: Gettier Paradoxes
summary: Agents can have justified true belief without knowledge in cleverly constructed cases.

name: General Voting Paradoxes
summary: Reasonable voting rules can exhibit spoilers, non-monotonicity, and other counterintuitive behaviors.

name: Alabama Paradox
summary: Increasing the total number of seats can cause a state to lose a seat under some apportionment rules.

name: Population Paradox
summary: A faster-growing state can lose a seat to a slower-growing state under certain apportionment methods.

name: Equity Premium Puzzle
summary: Observed equity returns appear too high relative to standard risk-aversion calibrations.

name: Giffen Paradox
summary: For some inferior goods, demand can rise when price rises because income effects dominate substitution effects.

name: Braess’s Paradox
summary: Adding a new route to a traffic network can worsen travel times at equilibrium.

name: Paradox of Free Choice
summary: In modal logic, disjunction introduction allows deriving contradictions from assumptions about free choice.

name: Paradox of Entailment
summary: From a contradiction, classical logic derives every statement, making inconsistent theories trivial.

name: Lottery Paradox
summary: Rationality requires believing each ticket loses but the conjunction that all lose is improbable.

name: Yablo's Paradox
summary: An infinite sequence of sentences where each says all later ones are false leads to liar-like contradiction without self-reference.

name: Skolem's Paradox
summary: Uncountable sets have countable models, challenging the absoluteness of countability.

name: Richard's Paradox
summary: A real number defined in finite English words is claimed undefinable, contradicting arithmetic.

name: Burali–Forti Paradox
summary: The set of all ordinals is an ordinal that does not contain itself.

name: Cantor's Paradox
summary: The set of all sets has no cardinality because its power set is strictly larger.

name: Wang's Paradox
summary: Vague predicates like 'heap' lead to contradictions when considering boundary cases.

name: Strengthened Liar Paradox

name: Revenge Liar Paradox
summary: Even after assigning truth predicates to levels, a new liar sentence at the next level arises.

name: No-No Paradox
summary: 'No one believes no one is infallible' creates scope ambiguities leading to contradiction.

name: All Horses are the Same Color
summary: Flawed induction 'proves' all horses share one color by overlapping sets.

name: Knower Paradox
summary: 'This sentence is unknown' combined with factive knowledge operator leads to inconsistency.

name: Unexpected Hanging Paradox
summary: A predicted surprise event cannot occur on any day, yet it does if unexpected.

name: Preface Paradox

name: Satisfaction Paradox

name: Self-Absorption Paradox

name: Rule-Following Paradox

name: Heterological Paradox
summary: Is 'heterological' (true of words not describing themselves) heterological?

name: KR Paradox
summary: Knowledge requires justified true belief, but justification loops create regress.

name: Variant Two-Envelope Paradoxes
summary: Infinite descent arguments in envelope switching using self-referential amounts.

name: Omniscience Paradox
summary: Can an omniscient being know contingent future choices without determining them?

name: Validity Paradox
summary: Valid arguments preserve truth, but soundness requires checking premises.

name: Boy or Girl Paradox
summary: Probability of boy given at least one boy changes based on sampling assumptions.

name: Borel–Kolmogorov Paradox
summary: Conditional probabilities on sets of measure zero depend on the conditioning method.

name: Borel's Paradox
summary: Conditional densities on the circle depend on coordinate choice despite rotational invariance.

name: False Positive Paradox
summary: Low-prevalence conditions make positive tests mostly false positives even with good accuracy.

name: Proebsting's Paradox

name: Will Rogers Phenomenon

name: Ant on a Rubber Rope

name: Hausdorff Paradox

name: Hooper's Paradox

name: Nikodym Set

name: Galileo's Paradox

name: Accuracy Paradox

name: Buffon's Needle Paradox

name: Thirders in Sleeping Beauty

name: Doomsday Argument

name: Ross-Littlewood Paradox

name: Galton Board Paradox

name: Friendship Paradox

name: Inspection Paradox

name: Google Interviews Paradox

name: 100 Prisoners Problem

name: Three Prisoners Problem

name: Quarterer Position

name: Sleeper Paradox

name: Bertrand's Ray Paradox

name: Margolus-Levitin Theorem Paradox

name: Earman-Baron Supertask

name: Leaning Tower of Pisa Paradox

name: Achilles and Arrow Paradox

name: Stadium Paradox

name: Secretary Problem Paradox
